sourceMovie.title: Badlands
sourceMovie.whyPeopleLoveIt: People revisit Badlands for the same reason it broke through in 1974: clear stakes, memorable faces, and a drama rhythm that doesn't stall in the middle. Terrence Malick keeps the ensemble pointed in one direction. The crime and drama blend is the engine: it lets the film shift registers without feeling like two different movies stitched together.

recommendations.title: The Wild One
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: If Badlands clicked for you, The Wild One (1953) is a strong next stop: both trade in crime and drama, with emotional whiplash that feels honest. Expect a different story, but a similar willingness to go big when the moment demands it.

recommendations.title: Thelma & Louise
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: If Badlands clicked for you, Thelma & Louise (1991) is a strong next stop: both trade in crime and drama, with momentum and clean stakes. Expect a different story, but a similar willingness to go big when the moment demands it.

faq.question: What should I watch after Badlands?
faq.answer: Try The Wild One, Thelma & Louise, The Quick and the Dead—each shares DNA with Badlands while changing the setting enough to feel fresh.

faq.question: What genre is Badlands?
faq.answer: Badlands (1974) is best described as Crime, Drama, Romance. Stores and algorithms use different labels, but that mix is what fans actually recognize on a rewatch.

faq.question: Is Badlands worth revisiting?
faq.answer: Viewer scores sit around 7.5/10 on TMDB—high enough that replays are common for people who like this crime lane.
